reviewNotes: This is the only explicit content requirement present in the supplied chunk; the detailed numbered content statements begin later in the specification.
The statement covers time, money, angle (degrees), metric/compound units and the practical use of rulers/protractors, so it is mapped to several unit and measurement nodes rather than a single node.
The specification states that any topic area may be assessed on any component at the relevant tier, so no fixed paper allocation is assigned.
Maps directly to integer four-operation and directed-number nodes.
Place value is required but not represented by a dedicated canonical node; other vocabulary maps cleanly.
Prime identification and prime-factor decomposition are covered by the primes and factors/multiples nodes.
HCF and LCM via listing and prime factorisation sit within factors and multiples.
Order-of-operations and bracket-use nodes match the statement.
No dedicated inverse-operations node; mapping captures the operations whose inverses are used.
Equivalent fractions and mixed-number conversion map to the corresponding fraction nodes.
The parent Fractions node is broader than the listed calculation skills.
Fraction-of-a-quantity and expressing one quantity as a fraction of another are narrower than the general Fractions node.
Terminating and recurring decimal-fraction conversions are covered exactly.
Decimal addition, subtraction and multiplication are instances of the four operations node.
Division of decimals by whole numbers or decimals sits under four operations.
Conversions between fractions, decimals and percentages are covered by the Percentages parent node.
Percentage-as-parts-per-hundred, percentage-of-a-quantity and comparing by percentage map exactly.
Percentage change, original-value problems and multiplier representation are covered.
The node covers ordering decimals; ordering fractions and percentages is not separately catalogued.
Comparison symbols have no dedicated node; closest coverage is ordering decimals and linear inequalities.
Positive, negative and fractional indices are mapped to their respective nodes.
Covers calculation of powers and roots, fractional powers, and estimation of powers and roots.
The laws of indices are captured by the parent Indices node.
Interpretation, ordering and conversion of standard form are covered.
Calculator and non-calculator calculations in standard form map to the calculating node.
Exact calculations with fractions and surds are covered; exact multiples of pi have no separate node.
Simplifying surds and rationalising denominators map exactly.
Rounding to decimal places, significant figures and powers of ten is covered.
Estimation of calculations by suitable approximation maps directly.
Error intervals, upper/lower bounds and bounds of discrete/continuous quantities are covered.
Equivalent ratios and 1:n form map to simplifying ratios.
Dividing a quantity in a given ratio and finding one quantity from another are covered.
Interpreting a part:part ratio as a fraction of the whole links ratio and fractions.
Ratio/proportion problem solving and the link to linear functions are partially covered.
Direct proportion including powers and roots is covered; power/root proportion is broader than the direct-proportion node.
No dedicated inverse-proportion node; proportional reasoning and reciprocal graphs provide the closest coverage.
Repeated percentage change, compound interest and exponential growth/decay formulae are covered.
Algebraic vocabulary, identity/equation distinction and proof construction are partially mapped.
Collecting like terms maps directly.
Simplifying algebraic products and quotients is narrower than the general algebraic manipulation node.
Single bracket, two binomials and more than two binomials expansions are covered.
Common factors and quadratics factorisation map to the factorisation node.
Completing the square on a quadratic expression maps directly.
No dedicated algebraic-fractions node; mapped to the broader algebraic manipulation node.
Formulating expressions and formulae from contexts maps directly.
Substituting positive or negative numbers into formulae and expressions maps directly.
Foundation column covers subject appearing once; Higher column adds subject appearing twice, powers and reciprocals.
Spans circle formulae, Pythagoras, right-angled trigonometry, quadratic formula and non-right-angled triangle formulae.
Uses the constant acceleration (SUVAT) equations.
Initial/foundation content covers solving, forming and contextual interpretation of linear equations.
Foundation adds knowledge of the formula; Higher adds rearrangement, completing the square and formula use.
Initial content is two linear simultaneous equations; Higher adds one linear/one quadratic.
Uses graphs to approximate roots/solutions and recognises intersection meaning.
Higher-tier sign-change methods (decimal search/interval bisection).
Foundation covers linear inequalities on a number line; Higher adds quadratic inequalities and set notation.
Linear inequalities in two variables represented graphically.
Function machines/inverse/composite interpreted without formal notation.
Covers term-to-term, position-to-term, nth-term rules including arithmetic and quadratic sequences.
Recognises special sequences; Fibonacci/other special cases have no dedicated node.
Coordinates in all four quadrants.
Progresses from linear/quadratic tables to other polynomials, reciprocals and exponentials.
Includes sketching simple polynomials/reciprocals, quadratic intercepts/turning points and roots.
Exponential graphs y = k^x for positive k.
Recognition and sketching of the three trigonometric graphs.
Circle centred at the origin.
Covers gradient/intercept, equations of lines and graphical inequality regions.
Parallel/perpendicular line equations and tangent to a circle; no dedicated perpendicular-lines node exists.
Translations and reflections of graphs without function notation.
Real-world graphs and direct/inverse proportion graphs.
Rates of change from gradients; instantaneous rate/chords/tangents are not separately represented.
Estimating areas under graphs in context; no general numerical-area node exists.
Basic geometrical terms for points, lines, planes, parallel and perpendicular.
Angle types and triangle side/angle labelling conventions.
Vocabulary for polygons, quadrilaterals and triangles.
Recognition of common 3D solids; cone and sphere are not in the supplied node catalogue.

syllabusPoints.unmappedReason: No canonical node for drawing diagrams from written descriptions.
reviewNotes: General diagram-drawing skill with no matching node.
Ruler/protractor use maps to measuring; compasses/circle construction is not represented.
Coordinates used in geometry and transformations.
Perpendicular bisector and midpoint construction.
Angle bisector construction.

syllabusPoints.unmappedReason: No canonical node for perpendicular from a point to a line or perpendicular at a point.
reviewNotes: Construction skill not represented in the node catalogue.

syllabusPoints.unmappedReason: No canonical node for loci or equidistant points.
reviewNotes: Loci constructions and real-world problems have no matching node.
Angle sum at a point and simple/formal proof applications.
Angles on a straight line sum to 180 degrees.
Vertically opposite, alternate and corresponding angles.
Interior/exterior angle sums and regular polygon angles, with proof applications.
Properties of triangles used in simple and formal proofs.
Properties of quadrilaterals used in simple and formal proofs.
Reflectional and rotational symmetry of polygons.

syllabusPoints.unmappedReason: No canonical node for circle vocabulary (centre, radius, chord, diameter, tangent, arc, sector, segment).
reviewNotes: Nomenclature only; no matching vocabulary node exists.
Angle at centre theorem, proved and applied.
Angle in a semicircle theorem, proved and applied.
Angles in the same segment theorem, proved and applied.
Perpendicular bisector of chord theorem, proved and applied.
Tangent-radius theorem, proved and applied.
Alternate segment theorem, proved and applied.
Cyclic quadrilateral opposite angles theorem, proved and applied.
Recognition and properties of common 3D solids; cone and sphere not in catalogue.

syllabusPoints.unmappedReason: No canonical node for plans and elevations of 3D solids.
reviewNotes: Interpreting and constructing plans/elevations is not represented.
Reflections, including identifying mirror lines x=a, y=b and y=±x at Higher.
Rotation of shapes through multiples of 90 degrees about a centre. Mapping to canonical rotations node is exact. Depth inferred from command verbs.
Translation described by column vector spans both transformation and vector notation nodes.
Sequences of isometric transformations and invariance map to combined transformations node.
Identifying and proving congruent triangles maps to congruent shapes node. Higher tier proof cases raise depth to reasoning.
Applying congruent triangles in calculations and simple proofs is an application of the congruence node.
Vector arithmetic and use in geometric arguments/proofs spans vector operations and vector geometry problem nodes.
Representing 2D vectors as column vectors maps exactly to the column vectors node.
Identifying and proving similar triangles maps to similar shapes node.
Enlargement with whole-number, fractional and negative scale factors spans enlargement and negative scale factor nodes.
Similarity involving lengths, areas and volumes maps to length and area scale factor nodes.
Use and conversion of standard units for length, area, volume/capacity, mass, time and money spans multiple unit nodes.
Compound units speed, density and pressure map directly to the corresponding rate nodes.
Scale of maps and scale drawings relate to ratio/simplifying ratios; bearings have no direct canonical node.
Perimeter of rectilinear shapes maps to area and perimeter of rectangles node.
Circumference and arc length map to the corresponding circle mensuration nodes.
Perimeter of composite shapes maps broadly to area/perimeter of rectangles node.
Area of triangle including 1/2 ab sin C spans basic triangle area and sine-area formula nodes.
Area of parallelogram/rectangle maps exactly to the parallelograms node.
Area of trapezium maps exactly to the trapezia node.
Area of circle and sector area map to the corresponding circle nodes.
Area of composite shapes maps broadly to area/perimeter of rectangles node.
Surface area and volume of cuboids, prisms and cylinders map to the corresponding solid nodes.
Cones and spheres are not represented by specific leaf nodes; mapped to the surface area and volume parent node.
Surface area and volume of pyramids map exactly to the pyramids node.
Pythagoras theorem maps exactly to the Pythagoras node.
Trigonometric ratios in right-angled triangles span the three ratio nodes.
Exact trigonometric values at standard angles map exactly to the exact values node.
Sine rule maps exactly to the sine rule node.
Cosine rule maps exactly to the cosine rule node.
Probability scale and likelihood language map exactly to the probability scale node.

syllabusPoints.unmappedReason: No canonical node for relative frequency or experimental probability.
reviewNotes: Recording and analysing relative frequency from repeated experiments is not matched by a specific canonical node.

syllabusPoints.unmappedReason: No canonical node for relative frequency approaching theoretical probability.
reviewNotes: Relationship between relative frequency and theoretical probability is not matched by a specific canonical node.
Equally likely outcomes, combined events and expected frequencies span several basic probability nodes.
Sample spaces map exactly to the sample spaces node.
Systematic listing and product rule map to the enumeration of outcomes node.
Venn diagrams and set notation span probability Venn diagrams and set language/Venn diagram nodes.
Tree diagrams map exactly to the tree diagrams node.
Addition law for mutually exclusive events spans addition rule and mutually exclusive events nodes.
Multiplication law and conditional probability span multiplication, conditional and independent events nodes.
Populations, samples, random sampling and bias span the sampling data collection nodes.
Charts for categorical and numerical data map to bar charts, pie charts and pictograms; no dedicated vertical-line or time-series line-graph leaf exists.
Histograms and cumulative frequency graphs for grouped data map to the corresponding diagram nodes.
Summary statistics including grouped estimates, box plots and comparisons span several averages/spread and box plot nodes.
Graphical misrepresentation maps partially to critiquing mathematical arguments node.
Scatter diagrams, correlation, causation and line of best fit span scatter diagram and correlation nodes.

syllabusPoints.unmappedReason: No canonical node for identifying or handling outliers.
reviewNotes: Outlier identification in data and on scatter graphs is not matched by a specific canonical node.
